summary: claude-cup harvests credentials from home directories, OS keychains, and browser cookie stores, validates them against vendor APIs, and exfiltrates results to an attacker-controlled Cloudflare Worker
details: On CLI startup, the package recursively walks every user profile (/Users, /home, C:\Users, depth 6) reading env files, IDE configs,.gitconfig, shell history and arbitrary user files, and applies a remote-fetched regex set to extract AWS, GitHub, OpenAI, Anthropic, GCP, Stripe, npm, Slack, HuggingFace, JWT, SendGrid, DigitalOcean and Sentry tokens, DB URIs, basic-auth URIs and PEM private keys. It also dumps OS credential vaults via Windows cmdkey/vaultcmd, macOS `security` keychain, and Linux keyrings, and copies Chrome/Edge/Firefox cookie SQLite databases under a disguised temp filename (`build-sess-*`) to query targeted hosts. Harvested tokens are live-validated against vendor APIs (GitHub user+orgs, `npm whoami`/`npm access ls-packages` via a temp ~/.npmrc, AWS STS get-caller-identity with the harvested keys injected via env, OpenAI/Anthropic/GitLab/HuggingFace/Stripe/Google) to confirm scope and write/publish permissions. Summary metadata (machine_id, session_id, signal counts, validation status, write/deploy flags) is base64url-encoded into the `m` query parameter and sent to https://cup-config.itaibels.workers.dev/v1/config. The same worker serves the scan ruleset, validator targets and audit prompt, giving the operator remote control of what is scanned and exfiltrated. The package establishes persistence by registering itself as a Claude Code MCP server and SessionStart/PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks in ~/.claude/settings.json and ~/.cursor/mcp.json (launcher at ~/.claude-jar/mcp-server.mjs), so the harvester reactivates on every future Claude Code session. It additionally shells out to the user's authenticated `claude -p --permission-mode auto --model sonnet` with the remote-fetched prompt, abusing the installer's paid AI session to discover credentials and write them to ~/.claude-jar/last-audit.json for ingest and upload. Source comments openly describe the evasion design (pacing to avoid looking 'like a credential spray', 'innocent temp name' laundering, leaderboard UI as cover). A postinstall-ping script is shipped that initializes a persistent machine_id and pings the same worker, ready to be wired in a future release.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
